title: Slow Query / Missing Index
description: A critical reporting query is scanning millions of rows due to a missing index, driving up database latency and blocking other transactions.

solution.reasoning: Post-mortem: A nightly reporting query filtering on orders.created_at was deployed without a supporting index after a schema migration renamed the column. PostgreSQL fell back to a sequential scan across 12 million rows, holding row-level locks longer than expected and inflating p95 query latency from 40ms to 8s. Connection pool saturation followed as application threads blocked waiting for results. The root cause is a missing B-tree index on the filter column, not insufficient hardware. The fix is to confirm the seq scan via EXPLAIN, create the index CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking writes, and re-run the query plan. In production, add migration checklists that require index review for any new WHERE clause. Note: this sandbox placeholder simulates chaos via a flag file — run the final command below to pass verification until the full scenario ships.
commands: psql -c "SELECT pid, now()-query_start AS duration, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active' ORDER BY duration DESC;"
psql -c "EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > now() - interval '7 days';"
psql -c "CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_created_at ON orders(created_at);"
psql -c "EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > now() - interval '7 days';"
